artworks.title: Flash Memory
artworks.description: A non-volatile memory that can retain data even without power.
artworks.details: Flash memory offers better resistance to shocks compared to traditional hard drives and is widely used in mobile devices.

artworks.title: Hard Disk Drive
artworks.description: A compact, high-capacity auxiliary storage device. The modern HDD architecture was established by the IBM 3340.
artworks.details: The company that developed the first HDD for desktop computers is now known as Seagate.

artworks.title: Optical Disc
artworks.artist: David Paul Gregg & James T. Russell
artworks.description: Emerged in the late 1960s as pioneers of digital format storage media, replacing analog formats.
artworks.details: Optical discs went through four generations: CD, DVD, Blu-ray Disc (BD), and Holographic Versatile Disc (HVD).

artworks.title: Floppy Disk
artworks.description: A once-ubiquitous storage device, with global usage exceeding 5 billion disks by the end of the 20th century.
artworks.details: The 3.5-inch floppy disk has a capacity of 1.44MB, which can only store one to four photos from a modern mobile phone.

artworks.title: Removable Magnetic Disk Pack
artworks.description: A removable disk pack used in RP03 disk drives with a storage capacity of 58MB.
artworks.details: Despite being referred to as 'removable,' this portable disk still weighs ten kilograms.

artworks.title: Magnetic Tape
artworks.description: A medium for storing audio, video, and computer data.
artworks.details: From 1980 to 2000, buying music albums on cassette tapes and renting movies from video tape rental stores was a nationwide phenomenon.

artworks.title: Punched Card
artworks.description: An early medium for programming, using pre-designed combinations of punches to represent each character.
artworks.details: The company Herman Hollerith established later became today's IBM.

artworks.title: JUKI 1300 Card-punch Machine
artworks.description: A machine to punch computer instructions or programs onto paper cards.
artworks.details: In the 1970s and 1980s, programming was commonly done offline using these machines.

artworks.title: DEC CR-11 Card Reader
artworks.description: A card reader used to execute instructions on mainframe computers from punched cards.
artworks.details: It was once involved in the computerized marking of the national university entrance exams in Taiwan.

artworks.title: Epson LX-800 9-Pin Dot Matrix Printer
artworks.description: A 9-pin dot matrix printer. These printers were among the earliest products to enter the market.
artworks.details: Dot matrix printers use impact pins to strike against an ink ribbon, transferring the ribbon's ink onto the paper.

artworks.title: 2nd Generation Chinese Keyboard and Input System
artworks.description: The world's first Chinese keyboard, born at NCTU.
artworks.details: It broke down each Chinese character into several 'Chinese radicals', overcoming the challenge of searching through a vast sea of characters.

artworks.title: Mouse
artworks.description: The world's first mouse, born at Stanford Research Institute (SRI).
artworks.details: The original was a small wooden box with a single button that could move forward, backward, left, and right.

artworks.title: iMac G3 Personal Computer
artworks.description: An iconic personal computer that consolidated the screen and system unit into a single enclosure.
artworks.details: Co-founder Steve Jobs once said that the iMac G3 was a 'computer that doesn't look like a computer!'

artworks.title: Compaq Portable 386
artworks.description: A pioneer in the field of 'portable computers', though it still weighed around ten kilograms.
artworks.details: It was priced between $12,000 to $14,000, making it expensive and primarily purchased by laboratories.

artworks.title: IBM PC-XT Personal Computer
artworks.description: An advanced model of the IBM PC designed for enterprise use, featuring a 10MB hard drive.
artworks.details: IBM's release of technical information (except the BIOS) led to a flourishing era of PC-compatible machines.

artworks.title: Apple II Personal Computer
artworks.description: The world's first widely popular personal computer, sold as 'home electronics'.
artworks.details: It became the standard computer in the American education system in the 1980s.

artworks.title: Convex C3 Mini-supercomputer
artworks.description: A mini-supercomputer optimized for vector processing, suitable for scientific research and engineering computations.
artworks.details: Arranged in a 'petal shape' to accelerate data transmission. Originally served at the National Center for High-Performance Computing.

artworks.title: WANG VS90 Minicomputer
artworks.description: A minicomputer specifically designed for document and data processing.
artworks.details: Widely used by businesses and government organizations like banks, railways, and newspapers.

artworks.title: CDC Cyber 170/720 Mainframe computer
artworks.description: A mainframe computer from the era of large-scale computing. It was used for the University Joint Entrance Examinations in Taiwan in 1981.
artworks.details: The CPU of the Cyber 170 series does not have I/O instructions; it relies on peripheral controllers.

artworks.title: CDC Cyber 170/720 Mainframe computer
artworks.description: A mainframe computer from the era of large-scale computing. It was used for the University Joint Entrance Examinations in Taiwan in 1981.
artworks.details: The CPU of the Cyber 170 series does not have I/O instructions; it relies on peripheral controllers.

artworks.title: CDC Cyber 170/720 Mainframe computer
artworks.description: A mainframe computer from the era of large-scale computing. It was used for the University Joint Entrance Examinations in Taiwan in 1981.
artworks.details: The CPU of the Cyber 170 series does not have I/O instructions; it relies on peripheral controllers.

artworks.title: DEC VAX Supermini Computer
artworks.description: A 32-bit supermini-computer that supports machine language and virtual addressing.
artworks.details: NCTU was an early adopter of the VAX series for information teaching and research.

artworks.title: LA30P DECwriter Terminal
artworks.description: A terminal designed to work with the PDP-11, it was a pioneer in dot matrix printing.
artworks.details: Users would input text using a keyboard, and the machine would print the output onto paper.

artworks.title: PDP-11 Minicomputer
artworks.description: A 16-bit minicomputer that was extremely popular, with over 600,000 units sold.
artworks.details: It utilized the Unibus memory bus to replace dedicated I/O instructions, a novel concept at the time.

artworks.title: RP03 Disk Drive
artworks.description: A removable disk drive commonly used with the PDP-10 or PDP-11 computers.
artworks.details: Despite its large size, similar to a washing machine, it had a relatively small storage capacity of only 58MB.

artworks.title: PDP-10 Mainframe Computer
artworks.description: A mainframe computer that revolutionized the industry with its time-sharing system.
artworks.details: It was the first large computer system in East Asia, purchased by NCTU in 1974.
